disease.name: Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD)
disease.note: Atherosclerotic occlusive disease of lower limb arteries. Ranges from asymptomatic to intermittent claudication to chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). In Kenya, PAD is underdiagnosed — patients often present late with rest pain or tissue loss. Diabetes and smoking are the dominant risk factors. The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is a simple bedside test that should be used more widely. CLTI carries high risk of limb loss and major cardiovascular events. Management requires risk factor modification, exercise, and revascularization when indicated.
presentingComplaints: Leg pain when walking that goes away with rest
Pain in the foot at night that gets better when hanging leg down
Sores or wounds on the foot that will not heal
Foot or toe turning black or blue
Cold feet even in warm weather
Pain in the buttock, thigh, or calf with walking

severityAssessment.severityLabel: CLTI (rest pain, tissue loss, gangrene) -> SEVERE; claudication (Rutherford 1-3) -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you get pain in your leg when walking? Where — calf, thigh, or buttock?
How far can you walk before the pain starts? (in meters or minutes)
Does the pain go away when you stop walking?
Do you have pain in your foot when lying in bed at night?
Does hanging your leg down over the side of the bed relieve the pain?
Do you have any sores or wounds on your foot that are slow to heal?
Have any of your toes turned black or blue?
Do you smoke tobacco or use khat/miraa? How many per day?
Do you have diabetes? How long? Is it controlled?
Do you have high blood pressure or high cholesterol?
Have you had a heart attack or stroke before?
Do you have any family history of heart disease or PAD?
Do you feel numbness or tingling in your feet?
Can you feel me touching your toes?
Do your feet feel cold compared to the rest of your body?
Have you noticed hair loss on your legs or shiny skin?
Do you have any pain in your arms or hands when doing tasks?
Have you had any surgery on your leg arteries before?
Do you take any blood thinners (aspirin, clopidogrel, warfarin)?
Do you have any kidney disease?
Do you have any known carotid artery narrowing or previous stroke/TIA?
Have you had any previous leg artery surgery or angioplasty?
Do you have erectile dysfunction (can be symptom of aortoiliac PAD - Leriche syndrome)?
Do you have pain in your legs at night that wakes you from sleep?
What do you do when the leg pain wakes you — do you hang your leg out of bed or walk around?
How many cigarettes do you smoke per day and for how many years?
Do you chew khat/miraa — how often and how long each session?
Have you ever been told you have weak or absent pulses in your feet?
Do you have any previous diagnosis of peripheral artery disease?
Do you have any family history of amputation due to diabetes or PAD?
Have you had any previous foot ulcers that healed slowly?
Do you walk barefoot at home or in the fields (farming)?
Have you seen a traditional healer for your leg problem?
Do you inspect your feet daily? (if diabetic)
Do you have any foot deformity (hammer toes, Charcot foot, bunions)?
Do you wear properly fitting shoes or open sandals?
Can you walk up a hill without stopping? (assess functional capacity)
Have you had any COVID-19 infection recently (can cause or worsen limb ischemia)?
Do you have any history of radiation therapy to your pelvis or groin (radiation arteritis)?
Do you take any anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy?
Have you ever had a positive HIV test? (HIV vasculopathy)
Do you have any history of TB treatment? (TB can cause vasculitis)
How would you describe the pain — cramping, burning, sharp, or aching?
Is the pain in one leg or both legs?

historyFeatures.note: Calf/buttock pain on walking, relieved by rest
Pain in forefoot at night, relieved by hanging leg down

examination_findings.finding: Absent or reduced femoral, popliteal, or pedal pulses (document which)
Cool or cold limb compared to contralateral
Pallor on leg elevation (>30 seconds)
Dependent rubor (reactive hyperemia when leg lowered)
Prolonged capillary refill time (>5 seconds)
Trophic skin changes: hair loss, shiny skin, thickened nails
Non-healing ulcer — typically on toes, heel, or pressure points
Gangrene — dry black necrotic tissue on toes or foot
Bruit over stenotic artery (femoral, popliteal)
Muscle atrophy of the calf or thigh
ABI <0.9 (claudication) or <0.4 (CLTI)
Buerger's test: foot becomes pale on elevation, then rubor on dependency
Diabetic foot exam: check sensation with monofilament (neuropathy)
Venous refill time (normal <20 seconds) — delayed in PAD
Soft tissue infection or pus around ulcer
Foot deformities: hammer toes, claw toes, Charcot foot (diabetic neuroarthropathy)
Examine all foot web spaces — look for fissures, maceration, fungal infection (portal for infection)
Check between toes for ulcers — often missed in a quick exam
Callus or corn formation over pressure points (precursor to ulceration)
Nail changes: thickened, brittle, onychomycosis, ingrowing toenails
Loss of hair on toes and dorsum of foot (chronic ischemia)
Palpate popliteal and femoral artery aneurysms (associated with PAD)
Auscultation for carotid, abdominal, femoral bruits
Measure upper arm blood pressure bilaterally (subclavian stenosis suggests systemic atherosclerosis)
Fundoscopy for diabetic and hypertensive retinopathy (assess end-organ damage)
Neurological exam: monofilament test (10g) at 3-4 sites on each foot
Vibration sense testing with 128 Hz tuning fork at great toe and medial malleolus
Achilles reflex testing (absent in peripheral neuropathy)
Check for popliteal artery entrapment (pulse diminishes with active plantarflexion)

bedside_scores.name: Rutherford Classification for PAD

components.name: Stage 5: minor tissue loss (non-healing ulcer, focal gangrene)
Stage 6: major tissue loss (extending above TM level)

bedside_scores.interpretation: Stages 0-3: claudication — medical therapy + exercise; Stage 4-6: CLTI — urgent vascular referral, revascularization. ABI correlates: >0.9 normal, 0.5-0.9 claudication, <0.4 CLTI

subtypes.name: Intermittent Claudication (Rutherford 1-3)
subtypes.management: Best medical therapy: antiplatelet (Aspirin 75mg + Clopidogrel 75mg if high risk), high-dose statin (Atorvastatin 80mg daily), BP control (ACE-i/ARB), smoking cessation program. Supervised exercise program (walk 3x/week, 30-45 min, to near-maximal pain). Endovascular revascularization only if disabling claudication despite medical therapy. Surgical bypass if endovascular not feasible.

subtypes.name: Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI, Rutherford 4-6)
subtypes.management: Urgent vascular surgery referral. Duplex + CTA imaging. Revascularization: endovascular first (PTA ± stent) if anatomy suitable, or surgical bypass (femoro-popliteal, femoro-distal) if not. Wound care (debridement, dressings, offloading). Antibiotics for infection. Minor amputation (toe/transmetatarsal) if non-salvageable. Major amputation (BKA/AKA) if unsalvageable. Cardiovascular risk reduction lifelong.

bedside.name: Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI)
bedside.indication: Gold standard bedside test: normal 0.9-1.3; claudication 0.5-0.9; CLTI <0.4; calcified vessels may give falsely high ABI (>1.3)

bedside.name: Segmental limb pressures (thigh, calf, ankle)
bedside.indication: Localize level of occlusion — pressure drop >20 mmHg between segments suggests stenosis

bedside.name: Toe-brachial index (TBI) or toe pressure
bedside.indication: Useful when ABI is falsely elevated due to vessel calcification (diabetes, CKD) — normal >0.7, CLTI <0.3

bedside.name: Transcutaneous oximetry (TcPO2)
bedside.indication: Tissue perfusion — TcPO2 <30 mmHg indicates CLTI; predicts wound healing

bedside.name: Buerger's test (elevation pallor + dependent rubor)
bedside.indication: Quick bedside assessment of vascular sufficiency

bedside.name: Pulse examination and documentation (0-3 scale)
bedside.indication: Femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial

bedside.name: Neurological exam (monofilament, vibration, reflexes)
bedside.indication: Screen for diabetic neuropathy — affects wound healing and symptoms

laboratory.name: Fasting lipid profile (total, LDL, HDL, triglycerides)
laboratory.indication: Assess cardiovascular risk — LDL target <1.4 mmol/L (<55 mg/dL)

laboratory.name: HbA1c
laboratory.indication: Diabetes assessment and control — target <7% (53 mmol/mol)

laboratory.name: Creatinine, eGFR
laboratory.indication: Renal function — contrast risk for imaging, prognosis

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Anemia worsens claudication, leukocytosis suggests infection

laboratory.name: CRP, ESR
laboratory.indication: Screen for vasculitis if young patient with no risk factors

laboratory.name: Fasting glucose
laboratory.indication: Screen for diabetes if not known

imaging.name: Arterial Duplex Ultrasound (lower limb runoff)
imaging.indication: First-line imaging — identifies stenosis, occlusion, velocities, and localizes disease segments

imaging.name: CT Angiography (Lower Limb Runoff)
imaging.indication: Excellent anatomical detail — pre-intervention planning, assesses inflow and outflow

imaging.name: Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)
imaging.indication: Gold standard, invasive — combined with endovascular intervention

imaging.name: MRI Angiography (MRA)
imaging.indication: Alternative to CTA if contrast allergy or renal impairment; may overestimate stenosis

management.protocolReference: ESC/ESVS Guidelines for Peripheral Arterial Disease (2024)

conservative: For claudication (Rutherford 1-3): best medical therapy + supervised exercise
Smoking cessation: single most effective intervention — reduces claudication progression, CV events, and mortality
Antiplatelet: Aspirin 75-100mg daily (all PAD patients); if high risk or prior revascularization: Aspirin + Clopidogrel 75mg daily
Statin: Atorvastatin 80mg daily (or Rosuvastatin 20mg) — target LDL <1.4 mmol/L
BP control: ACE-i or ARB first-line, target <130/80 mmHg
Supervised exercise: walk 30-45 min, 3-5x/week, to near-maximal pain, rest, repeat
analgesia: For rest pain: Paracetamol + weak opioids (Tramadol)
Leg dependency (hanging leg down) provides relief
Avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or on anticoagulation

endovascular: Endovascular revascularization first-line for most CLTI and disabling claudication
PTA (percutaneous transluminal angioplasty) ± stent for iliac, femoral, popliteal stenosis
Drug-coated balloon (DCB) for SFA disease to reduce restenosis
Drug-eluting stent (DES) for SFA and popliteal
Below-knee PTA for tibial disease in CLTI
surgical: Surgical bypass: for failed endovascular, extensive disease, or young active patients
Femoro-popliteal bypass (above or below knee) with vein (GSV) or synthetic graft (PTFE) for above-knee
Femoro-distal bypass (to tibial or pedal vessels) — with vein only
Endarterectomy: for localized common femoral or deep femoral stenosis
Amputation: for unsalvageable CLTI (toe, transmetatarsal, below-knee, above-knee)

general: Check distal pulses and Doppler signals immediately post-procedure
Monitor for acute occlusion, bleeding, hematoma at access site
Dual antiplatelet therapy (Aspirin + Clopidogrel) for at least 1 month after stenting, then single
Statin and BP control continued lifelong
bypass_surgery: Monitor graft patency: duplex at 1 month, 6 months, 12 months, then annually
Antiplatelet: Aspirin 75mg lifelong (± Clopidogrel for 1 month)
wound_care: If CLTI with tissue loss: debridement of non-viable tissue, wound dressings, offloading
Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) for complex wounds
Podiatry referral for ongoing foot care
major_amputation: Below-knee amputation (BKA) preferred over above-knee (AKA) — better rehabilitation
Stump care: rigid dressing or soft dressing, monitor for infection

pathways.criteria: exertional leg pain, ABI 0.5-0.9, no rest pain, no tissue loss
pathways.treatment: Best medical therapy: smoking cessation, antiplatelet, high-dose statin, BP control, supervised exercise. Revascularization only if disabling claudication despite optimal medical therapy.

pathways.criteria: rest pain >2 weeks, non-healing ulcer, gangrene, ABI <0.4
pathways.treatment: Urgent vascular referral. Duplex/CTA imaging. Revascularization (endovascular first or bypass). Wound debridement and care. Antibiotics if infected. Minor amputation if non-salvageable digital. Major amputation if unsalvageable limb.

operative_templates.indication: SFA occlusion not amenable to endovascular treatment, failed endovascular, CLTI with SFA disease
operative_templates.approach: Longitudinal groin incision + medial thigh incision above knee
operative_templates.position: Supine with leg slightly abducted and externally rotated, knee slightly flexed
operative_templates.incision: Groin incision over femoral pulse + medial thigh incision from mid-thigh to adductor hiatus
steps: Harvest great saphenous vein (GSV) from ipsilateral or contralateral leg — reversed for bypass
Groin incision: isolate common femoral, profunda femoris, and SFA
Above-knee incision: identify above-knee popliteal artery (anterior to popliteal vein)
Create tunnel (subsartorial) between the two incisions
Proximal anastomosis: end-to-side to common femoral artery with 5-0 or 6-0 Prolene
Distal anastomosis: end-to-side to above-knee popliteal artery with 6-0 Prolene
Check flow with Doppler, check distal pulses
Close wounds in layers with drains
keyLandmarks: Common femoral artery — bifurcates into SFA and profunda, above inguinal ligament becomes external iliac
Popliteal artery (above knee) — in adductor canal, anterior to popliteal vein
Saphenous vein — in subcutaneous tissue of medial thigh and leg

watchFor: clear fluid draining from groin wound
new claudication or rest pain in other leg

dischargeCriteria: Distal pulses (or Doppler signals) present and improved compared to pre-op
Pain controlled on oral analgesia — no rest pain
Wounds clean, dry, no signs of infection
Antiplatelet and statin therapy prescribed and patient educated
Wound care plan (if CLTI) established with clear instructions
Follow-up vascular clinic appointment within 2 weeks

patient_education.explanation: Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD) is narrowing or blockage of the arteries in your legs due to the build-up of fatty deposits (atherosclerosis). This reduces blood flow to your leg muscles and feet. In early stages, you get pain in your legs when walking (claudication). In more advanced stages, you have pain at rest and may develop wounds that do not heal (CLTI). PAD is also a warning sign that you have atherosclerosis elsewhere — you have a higher risk of heart attack and stroke.
warningSigns: Leg pain at rest or at night that wakes you up
Sores or wounds on your feet that do not heal within 2 weeks
Toe or foot turning black or blue
Sudden worsening of leg pain, coldness, or numbness (acute ischemia — emergency)
Chest pain or shortness of breath (heart attack or PE signs)
activity: Walk daily — push through the pain until it becomes moderate, then rest and repeat
Walking 30-45 minutes daily, 3-5 times per week, improves collateral circulation
Stop smoking immediately — this is the single most effective treatment
Inspect your feet daily — especially if you have diabetes
Wear well-fitting shoes and never walk barefoot
diet: Heart-healthy diet: low saturated fat, low salt, high in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
If diabetic: strict dietary control, monitor blood sugar
medication: Aspirin 75mg daily for life — prevents heart attack, stroke, and leg complications
Atorvastatin 80mg daily — lowers cholesterol and reduces cardiovascular events
Blood pressure medications as prescribed — target <130/80 mmHg
If diabetic: take diabetes medications as directed, target HbA1c <7%
Do NOT stop medications without consulting your doctor
followUp: Regular follow-up at vascular clinic — every 6-12 months
ABI and Duplex surveillance to monitor disease progression
After bypass/stenting: regular graft surveillance with ultrasound
Annual cardiovascular risk assessment (ECG, lipid profile, HbA1c)

before_discharge: Medication counseling — importance of antiplatelet, statin, BP control
Smoking cessation — offer referral to quit program
Foot care education — daily inspection, moisturize, proper footwear
Wound care instructions if CLTI with tissue loss

after_discharge.action: Wound check, symptom assessment, medication review
Clinical review — walking distance, ABI, risk factor control
Duplex surveillance, lipid profile, HbA1c, BP check
Annual vascular review — ABI, duplex, cardiovascular risk assessment
Annual surveillance if stable; more frequent if CLTI or after bypass

when_to_return: Leg pain at rest or at night
New or worsening leg ulcer or wound
Leg becomes cold, pale, numb, or suddenly painful
Chest pain or shortness of breath
Fever with redness or wound discharge

pathophysiologyDetail: PAD is a manifestation of systemic atherosclerosis: atherosclerotic plaques in the infrarenal aorta, iliac, femoral, popliteal, and tibial arteries cause hemodynamically significant stenosis (>50% diameter reduction, >75% cross-sectional area reduction) producing a pressure drop across the lesion

Endothelial dysfunction is the initiating event: reduced nitric oxide bioavailability → increased endothelial permeability to LDL → LDL oxidation in the intima → monocyte recruitment (MCP-1, VCAM-1) → macrophage foam cell formation → fatty streak → fibrous plaque

Plaque progression: smooth muscle cell migration from media to intima → proliferation under PDGF and FGF stimulation → extracellular matrix deposition (collagen, proteoglycans) → formation of a fibrous cap over a lipid-rich necrotic core. Unequal plaque growth into the lumen creates complex, eccentric stenoses

Hemodynamic effect of stenosis: at rest, a >50% stenosis produces a pressure gradient. During exercise, vasodilation of distal arterioles reduces resistance → increased flow velocity across the stenosis → greater pressure drop (the 'steal' phenomenon) → calf muscle ischemia during exertion (claudication) → pain with walking, relief with rest

Collateral circulation: chronic ischemia stimulates VEGF and bFGF release → formation of collateral vessels (arteriogenesis). Well-developed collaterals can restore up to 80% of resting flow but cannot meet exercise demand. Diabetic patients have impaired collateral formation (reduced VEGF response, endothelial dysfunction)

Critical limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI): when multi-level occlusive disease (aorto-iliac + femoropopliteal + tibial) produces flow insufficient for resting metabolic demands → rest pain (forefoot, worse at night, relieved by dependency) → tissue loss (ulceration, gangrene). The ankle pressure <50 mmHg or toe pressure <30 mmHg defines CLTI

Atherothromboembolism: plaque rupture exposes thrombogenic core → platelet aggregation → mural thrombus → embolization to distal vessels (cholesterol emboli syndrome: blue toe, livedo reticularis, acute kidney injury) or acute on chronic ischemia if in-situ thrombosis occurs at a critical stenosis

Diabetic PAD has distinct features: more distal (tibial/peroneal with sparing of the foot arteries), more diffuse, more calcified (medial calcinosis — Monckeberg's arteriosclerosis → incompressible vessels, falsely elevated ABIs). Diabetic patients have 3-5x higher amputation risk than non-diabetics due to combined PAD and neuropathy

In Kenya, PAD affects 5-10% of adults >50 years (similar to global estimates) but is underdiagnosed. Diabetes prevalence in Kenya is rising (approximately 5% of adults) and is the strongest risk factor. Peripheral neuropathy from diabetes or HIV (or TB treatment) masks PAD symptoms — many patients present with CLTI (tissue loss) as the first manifestation rather than claudication

Smoking and farming-related PAD: Kenya has high rates of tobacco use (10-15% of adults). Farmers who also smoke have a particularly high PAD burden because prolonged walking in bare feet (not noticing ischemic pain) + neuropathy + atherosclerosis → foot ulcers that are treated late by traditional healers. Calcaneal pressure ulcers are a classic presentation

HIV and PAD: HIV infection increases PAD risk 2-3x through chronic inflammation (elevated CRP, IL-6), dyslipidemia (ART-related), and direct arterial wall infection. Protease inhibitors worsen metabolic profile. Kenyan HIV patients on ART for >5 years have a 10-15% prevalence of PAD

The 'critical stenosis' concept: a stenosis is hemodynamically significant when it reduces the cross-sectional area by >75% (>50% diameter reduction by angiography). At this point, resting blood flow is reduced. Below this threshold, resting flow is normal but exercise-induced hyperemia is impaired (claudication). A >90% stenosis produces resting ischemia. This explains why ABI measurements at rest can be normal in claudicants — the stenosis is 'critical' only during exercise. Exercise ABI (walking for 1 minute at 2 mph on a 12% incline) reveals the pressure drop: a post-exercise ABI drop >30% confirms PAD

NO bioavailability and endothelial function: in PAD, endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) is uncoupled (becomes a superoxide generator instead of NO producer) due to oxidative stress. Asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA) — an endogenous NOS inhibitor — is elevated in PAD patients. NO deficiency causes: (1) impaired endothelium-dependent vasodilation → reduced exercise-induced hyperemia; (2) increased platelet aggregation and adhesion → atherothrombosis; (3) increased leukocyte adhesion → inflammation. This is why exercise training (which upregulates eNOS and antioxidant enzymes) is therapeutic — it restores NO bioavailability

Skeletal muscle changes in CLTI are not just ischemic: chronic ischemia leads to myocyte apoptosis, mitochondrial dysfunction, fiber type switching (Type I → Type II — more glycolytic, less oxidative), and satellite cell depletion. This 'ischemic myopathy' explains why revascularized limbs with restored blood flow still have poor function — the muscle has already undergone irreversible damage. Earlier revascularization preserves muscle function. The concept of 'metabolic myopathy of PAD' is a frontier area of research — no approved therapies yet exist to reverse it

Inflammatory biomarkers in PAD: elevated CRP (>3 mg/L) independently predicts MACE and MALE in PAD patients. IL-6 is elevated in CLTI and correlates with amputation risk. Fibrinogen level >400 mg/dL is associated with 2x increased graft occlusion risk. D-dimer >1000 ng/mL predicts progression to CLTI. These biomarkers are not yet part of clinical decision-making but can help risk-stratify patients. In Kenya, CRP and D-dimer are available at relatively low cost and should be used more widely to identify high-risk PAD patients who need aggressive medical therapy

The role of revascularization in modifying the natural history: successful revascularization in CLTI improves amputation-free survival beyond just limb salvage — it reduces mortality by 15-20% at 5 years compared to failed revascularization (BASIL Trial). The mechanism is not entirely clear but likely involves: (1) restored mobility → reduced cardiovascular deconditioning; (2) improved wound healing → reduced infection, sepsis; (3) better tolerance of antiplatelet and statin therapy. This creates a compelling argument for aggressive revascularization in CLTI — it is not just limb salvage but life prolonging

landmarkTrials.trial: COMPASS Trial (Rivaroxaban + Aspirin vs Aspirin for PAD)
landmarkTrials.population: 27,395 patients with stable atherosclerotic disease (7,470 with PAD) randomized to rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin 100 mg daily vs aspirin alone vs rivaroxaban 5 mg BID alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban + aspirin reduced MACE (CVD mortality, MI, stroke) by 24% vs aspirin (HR 0.76, p<0.001) and MALE (major adverse limb events — amputation, revascularization) by 46% (HR 0.54, p<0.01). Major bleeding increased (3.1% vs 1.9%, p<0.001) but ICH and fatal bleeding were similar
landmarkTrials.impact: Established rivaroxaban + aspirin as a superior antithrombotic strategy for high-risk PAD patients. Changed guidelines worldwide
landmarkTrials.reference: Eikelboom JW, et al. COMPASS Trial. N Engl J Med. 2017;377(14):1319-1330

landmarkTrials.trial: VOYAGER-PAD Trial (Rivaroxaban + Aspirin After Lower Extremity Revascularization)
landmarkTrials.population: 6,564 patients with PAD undergoing lower extremity revascularization randomized to rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin vs aspirin alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban + aspirin reduced the composite of acute limb ischemia, major amputation, MI, stroke, or CVD death by 15% (HR 0.85, p=0.009). Acute limb ischemia reduction: 33% (HR 0.67). TIMI major bleeding increased (2.65% vs 1.82%, HR 1.43, p=0.07) but no excess ICH or fatal bleeding
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed the COMPASS findings in post-revascularization PAD patients. Standard of care for PAD patients after revascularization
landmarkTrials.reference: Bonaca MP, et al. VOYAGER-PAD. N Engl J Med. 2020;382(22):2116-2127

landmarkTrials.trial: BASIL Trial (Bypass vs Angioplasty for Severe Ischemia of the Leg)
landmarkTrials.population: 452 patients with CLTI randomized to infrapopliteal angioplasty (PTA) vs open bypass surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: Amputation-free survival at 2 years: 56% surgery vs 57% angioplasty (p=0.85). Beyond 2 years, surgery had better amputation-free survival (HR 0.37, p=0.03). Hospital stay: longer for surgery (17 vs 10 days). Costs: similar at 2 years
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark trial establishing equipoise between PTA and bypass for CLTI. Advocated for an 'endovascular-first' approach for patients with life expectancy <2 years, surgery-first for longer life expectancy

landmarkTrials.trial: BASIL-2 Trial (vein bypass vs endovascular-first for CLTI due to infrapopliteal disease)
landmarkTrials.population: 345 patients with CLTI due to infrapopliteal disease randomized to vein bypass vs endovascular therapy first
landmarkTrials.finding: Amputation-free survival at 3 years: 57% vein bypass vs 66% endovascular-first (HR 1.35, p=0.11 — trend favoring endovascular). Major amputation: similar (21% bypass vs 17% endo, p=0.50). Mortality: higher in bypass group (32% vs 23%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the 'bypass first' dogma for infrapopliteal CLTI. Endovascular-first may be preferred, with bypass reserved for failed endovascular therapy
landmarkTrials.reference: Bradbury AW, et al. BASIL-2 Trial. Lancet. 2023;401(10285):1193-1204

landmarkTrials.trial: EUCLID Trial (Ticagrelor vs Clopidogrel in PAD)
landmarkTrials.population: 13,885 patients with symptomatic PAD randomized to ticagrelor (90 mg BID) vs clopidogrel (75 mg daily)
landmarkTrials.finding: No difference in MACE (HR 1.02, p=0.65) or MALE (HR 0.93, p=0.41). Bleeding similar (HR 1.10, p=0.25). Ticagrelor has no advantage over clopidogrel in PAD
landmarkTrials.impact: Clopidogrel remains the P2Y12 inhibitor of choice for PAD (cost advantage too — clopidogrel is significantly cheaper)
landmarkTrials.reference: Hiatt WR, et al. EUCLID Trial. N Engl J Med. 2017;376(14):1334-1343

landmarkTrials.trial: Hankey GJ, et al. Systematic review of ticlodipine/clopidogrel vs aspirin in PAD (CAPRIE substudy)
landmarkTrials.population: 19,185 patients with atherosclerotic vascular disease (6,452 with PAD) randomized to clopidogrel vs aspirin
landmarkTrials.finding: Clopidogrel reduced CV events by 23.8% (p=0.0028) in the PAD subgroup vs aspirin. Overall population: 8.7% RRR (p=0.043)
landmarkTrials.impact: Clopidogrel has greater benefit in PAD than in CAD or CVD. PAD patients derive the greatest relative benefit from P2Y12 inhibition

landmarkTrials.trial: CLEVER Trial (Claudication: Exercise vs Endoluminal Revascularization)
landmarkTrials.population: 111 patients with aortoiliac PAD and claudication randomized to supervised exercise (SET), stent revascularization, or combined SET + stenting, vs optimal medical care (OMC) alone
landmarkTrials.finding: At 6 months: SET improved peak walking time by 5.8 min vs OMC (p<0.001), stenting improved by 3.7 min (p=0.022), combined improved by 5.1 min. Disease-specific QoL: stenting was better at 6 months. Walking economy and daily activity: SET was superior. No difference in ABI improvement at 6 months
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark trial comparing lifestyle (exercise) vs interventional (stenting) in claudication. SET is superior for functional outcomes; stenting is better for QoL. Combined approach may offer best results. Patient preference and resource availability should guide treatment
landmarkTrials.reference: Murphy TP, et al. CLEVER Trial. Circulation. 2011;125(1):130-139

landmarkTrials.trial: MIMIC Trial (Moderate Intermittent Claudication — Supervised vs Unsupervised Exercise)
landmarkTrials.population: 83 patients with intermittent claudication randomized to supervised treadmill exercise vs unsupervised home-based exercise vs usual care
landmarkTrials.finding: Supervised exercise improved maximal walking distance by 150% (vs 30% in unsupervised, p<0.001). Pain-free walking distance improved by 120% supervised vs 25% unsupervised. Supervised was 3x more expensive but 5x more effective. Home-based exercise was minimally effective
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that supervised exercise therapy (SET) is far superior to 'go home and walk' advice. SET programs are cost-effective and should be the standard first-line treatment for claudication. Unsupervised advice is largely ineffective — patients do not walk enough or at sufficient intensity
landmarkTrials.reference: Cheetham DR, et al. MIMIC Trial. Br J Surg. 2008;95(9):1115-1121

landmarkTrials.trial: ICAI Trial (Iloprost vs Aspirin in Critical Leg Ischemia — Italian Multicenter)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,560 patients with CLTI not suitable for revascularization randomized to iloprost (prostacyclin analogue) IV infusion vs aspirin
landmarkTrials.finding: At 6 months: iloprost reduced composite endpoint (death, major amputation, no clinical improvement) by 18% (p=0.022). Amputation rate: lower in iloprost group (37% vs 45%, p=0.015). More side effects: headache, nausea, flushing (withdrawn in 15%). Benefit was most pronounced in patients with ischemic ulcers
landmarkTrials.impact: Prostanoids (iloprost) are effective for CLTI when revascularization is not possible. Now recommended in guidelines for 'no-option' CLTI (TASC D, poor runoff). Not widely used in Kenya due to cost and IV administration requirement. Oral prostacyclin analogs have not shown benefit

landmarkTrials.trial: Meta-analysis: Heated debate — Statin therapy reduces CV events and improves walking distance in PAD (Heart Protection Study and subsequent)
landmarkTrials.population: Pooled analysis of 20,536 patients with PAD from HPS and other statin trials
landmarkTrials.finding: Statin therapy reduced major vascular events by 22% (HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.72-0.84) in PAD patients. Walking distance improved by 15-50% (eventual claudication distance increased by 50m, p<0.001). Mortality reduction at 5 years: 15% for PAD patients on statins
landmarkTrials.impact: Statins are the single most effective medical therapy for PAD — they reduce CV events, improve walking distance, and reduce mortality. Every PAD patient should be on a high-intensity statin regardless of baseline cholesterol. The LDL-independent pleiotropic benefits (anti-inflammatory, endothelial function improvement) may be as important as lipid lowering
landmarkTrials.reference: Heart Protection Study Collaborative Group. Lancet. 2002;360(9326):7-22; TASC II, JVS 2007

evidenceTable.recommendation: ABI screening for all patients with diabetes >50 years, smokers >50 years, or leg symptoms suggestive of PAD — ABI <0.90 = PAD
Supervised exercise therapy (SET) is first-line for claudication — 30-45 min, 3x/week for 12 weeks — improves pain-free walking distance by 50-200%
evidenceTable.reference: Lane R, et al. Cochrane 2017; AHA/ACC Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Anti-platelet therapy: aspirin 75-100 mg daily or clopidogrel 75 mg daily for all symptomatic PAD patients
Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin 100 mg daily for high-risk PAD (CLTI, prior revascularization, polyvascular disease) — reduces MACE and MALE
High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg) — reduces MACE, MALE, and improves walking distance
Smoking cessation reduces PAD progression and CV events. Varenicline or nicotine replacement should be offered at every visit
Cilostazol 100 mg BID improves walking distance in claudication (50-100% increase). Contraindicated in heart failure
evidenceTable.reference: Thompson PD, et al. JACC 2005; NICE Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Revascularization (endovascular or surgical) is indicated for CLTI or disabling claudication after failed SET
evidenceTable.reference: BASIL Trial, Lancet 2005; BASIL-2, Lancet 2023; ESVS Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Glycemic control (HbA1c <7%) reduces microvascular complications but data for PAD-specific benefit is less robust
evidenceTable.reference: UKPDS; ACCORD; VADT — diabetic PAD substudies

evidenceTable.recommendation: Great saphenous vein (GSV) is the preferred conduit for infrainguinal bypass (vs PTFE) — 80% vs 60% 5-year patency
evidenceTable.reference: Veith FJ, et al. JVS 1986; Klinkert P, et al. Cochrane 2004

evidenceTable.recommendation: Supervised exercise therapy (SET) is superior to 'go home and walk' advice for claudication. SET improves pain-free walking distance by 120-150%
evidenceTable.reference: MIMIC Trial, Br J Surg 2008; CLEVER Trial, Circulation 2011

evidenceTable.recommendation: Drug-coated balloons (DCB) and drug-eluting stents (DES) reduce restenosis and target lesion revascularization compared to plain balloon angioplasty in SFA disease
evidenceTable.reference: Zilver PTX, JACC 2011; IN.PACT SFA, JACC 2015; AHA/ACC 2024

evidenceTable.recommendation: In CLTI patients with infrapopliteal disease, endovascular-first approach is preferred over vein bypass (BASIL-2 showed trend favoring endovascular). Bypass is reserved for failed endovascular therapy
Iloprost (IV prostacyclin) may be considered for 'no-option' CLTI patients who are not candidates for revascularization — reduces amputation risk by 20%
Foot care education, daily foot inspection, and appropriate footwear reduce ulceration and amputation risk in diabetic PAD patients by 50-70%
evidenceTable.reference: Armstrong DG, Boulton AJM, N Engl J Med 2017; IDF Guidelines 2023

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Leg pain on walking (claudication) OR rest pain/tissue loss (CLTI) → Check ABI: Normal >1.0, Borderline 0.91-1.0, Abnormal <0.90 (<0.50 = severe, <0.30 = CLTI). If ABI >1.4 (incompressible calcified vessels) → measure toe-brachial index (TBI): normal >0.70, <0.50 = PAD, <0.30 = CLTI. → If claudication: confirm with exercise ABI (post-1-min walking ABI drop >30% confirms PAD). Ankle duplex: localize stenosis (iliac, femoral, popliteal, tibial). → If CLTI (rest pain/tissue loss): urgent vascular consultation. CTA or MRA of aorta to foot: identify multi-level occlusive disease, inflow (aorto-iliac) and outflow (femoropopliteal-tibial). Toe pressures <30 mmHg = CLTI. → Management by Rutherford category: 0-1 (asymptomatic): risk factor modification. 2-3 (claudication): SET, cilostazol, risk factor modification. 4 (rest pain): revascularization (endovascular vs open). 5-6 (tissue loss): urgent revascularization + amputation if non-salvageable. → Pre-revascularization workup: CTA runoff (preferred) or DSA. Cardiac risk assessment (METs >4, no unstable CAD → proceed). → Revascularization choice: Aorto-iliac (TASC A-C): endovascular (stenting). TASC D: open (aortobifemoral bypass). Femoropopliteal: endovascular for short lesions <25 cm (drug-coated balloon or stent), open for long occlusions (fem-pop bypass with vein). Tibial: endovascular or bypass. → Long-term: antiplatelet (clopidogrel or rivaroxaban + aspirin), high-intensity statin, smoking cessation, DM control, exercise, surveillance duplex at 1, 6, 12 months. ● In Kenya: ABI is the most accessible diagnostic tool. CTA/MRA is limited to major centers. Amputation rates are high (3-5x Western centers) due to late CLTI presentation. Many patients choose traditional healers over surgery. Cost of EVT/stents is prohibitive — open bypass (with harvested saphenous vein) is more practical and affordable. Post-operative supervision (wound care, diabetic foot care) is a major challenge in rural areas.

managementPearls.context: The claudication that is not claudication
managementPearls.pearl: Not all leg pain on walking is PAD. Spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication) presents with bilateral leg pain/burning/weakness on standing/walking, RELIEVED by leaning forward (lumbar flexion) or sitting. True vascular claudication: cramping calf pain that comes after a FIXED distance, relieved by standing still (not sitting/leaning). Check: can the patient ride a stationary bike? Neurogenic claudication: able to bike (lumbar flexion), not able to walk. Vascular: same walking distance regardless of position. And always check ABI — it costs almost nothing and confirms the diagnosis.

managementPearls.context: Toe pressures are your friend in diabetes
managementPearls.pearl: In diabetic PAD, medial calcinosis makes ankle arteries incompressible → ABI is falsely elevated (>1.4). In these patients, the ABI is useless. Always measure toe pressures (TBI) — digital arteries are spared from calcinosis. TBI <0.5 = CLTI in diabetes. Toe pressure <30 mmHg = requires revascularization. Many diabetic patients in Kenya present with a 'normal' ABI but a gangrenous toe — you need TBI to diagnose the severity.
managementPearls.evidence: Høyer C, Sandermann J, et al. J Diabetes Complications 2016; TASC II

managementPearls.pearl: In CLTI with tissue loss, do NOT rush to amputation. Even a 'dead-looking' foot can be salvaged. Key: assess the foot veins — if you can see a dorsal foot vein (indicating some inflow), and the foot is warm (indicating some perfusion), the foot may be salvageable. Get a CTA to identify a target vessel for bypass. The BASIL trial showed that attempted revascularization before amputation improves survival. Amputation should be the last resort, not the first.

Stop using Rutherford alone for CLTI. The WIfI (Wound, Ischemia, foot Infection) classification is more predictive of amputation risk and guides revascularization. W: wound depth/extent (0-3), I: ABI/TBI/TP (0-3), fI: infection severity (0-3). Total score: for each stage, estimate 1-year risk of amputation. WIfI stage 4: 50% amputation risk at 1 year. WIfI stage 1: <10%. Always document WIfI for CLTI patients — it drives treatment decisions and predicts outcomes.
managementPearls.evidence: Mills JL Sr, et al. WIfI Classification. JVS 2014

managementPearls.pearl: In PAD, foot care can prevent amputation. Key: daily foot inspection (patient or caregiver), dry between toes, moisturize (not between toes), cut toenails straight across, avoid barefoot walking, report any blister/cut/ulcer immediately. For patients with calluses: see a podiatrist. In Kenya, where barefoot walking is common and diabetic neuropathy + PAD coexists, injuries to the foot are common. Simple education about footwear (closed shoes, not thong sandals) can prevent ulcers that lead to amputation.

Every PAD patient should be on four pillars of medical therapy: (1) Antiplatelet: Aspirin 75 mg daily (clopidogrel 75 mg if intolerant, or rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin for high-risk patients per COMPASS). (2) High-intensity statin: Atorvastatin 80 mg or Rosuvastatin 20 mg — target LDL <1.4 mmol/L. (3) ACE inhibitor or ARB: Ramipril 10 mg or equivalent — reduces CV events beyond BP control. (4) Smoking cessation: varenicline or nicotine replacement. If the patient is only on aspirin (no statin, no BP med, no smoking cessation), they are NOT on optimal medical therapy and their risk of MI/stroke/amputation is 3-5x higher than necessary. In Kenya, these medications are all available and mostly affordable (aspirin ~$2/month, atorvastatin ~$10/month, ramipril ~$5/month) — the barrier is not cost but awareness and prescribing.

managementPearls.context: Amputation level decision — BKA vs AKA
managementPearls.pearl: BKA rehabilitation: patients walk with 30% more energy than AKA — the knee joint is critical for functional ambulation. Decision factors: (1) BKA healing requires adequate blood supply — assess with: TP >30 mmHg (ideal), StO2 >40%, bleeding wound edges at surgery. (2) Healed BKA → 70% prosthetic use, AKA → 40% prosthetic use. (3) But a BKA that fails to heal (wound breakdown) requires conversion to AKA — which is devastating and prolongs recovery by 6-12 months. (4) Even if the foot is unsalvageable, a BKA may still be possible if the calf skin is viable (BKA through viable tissue vs AKA for non-viable calf). (5) Rule: if you have any doubt about BKA healing, choose AKA. A healed AKA is better for the patient than a non-healing BKA that eventually becomes an AKA. In Kenya, where prosthetic services are limited and expensive, a well-healed BKA with a properly fitted prosthesis is transformative — but long-term prosthetic follow-up is often poor, so AKA may be more practical in some settings.
managementPearls.evidence: Taylor SM, et al. Amputation level. JVS 2005; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: CLTI wound care — the basics that save limbs
managementPearls.pearl: Wound care after revascularization is as important as the revascularization itself. Principles: (1) Debride all non-viable tissue (eschars, slough). Sharp debridement at bedside or in OR — dead tissue is a medium for infection. (2) Offload the wound — for heel ulcers, use a heel-protection boot. For forefoot ulcers, use a wedge shoe or crutches. No pressure on the ischemic wound = healing. (3) Moist wound healing — do not let the wound dry out. Use hydrogels (for dry slough), foams (moderate exudate), alginates (heavy exudate). Change 1-2x per week. (4) Antiseptics: avoid povidone-iodine, hydrogen peroxide, alcohol — they damage granulation tissue. Normal saline is best. (5) Infection: treat aggressively — wound swab, culture-guided antibiotics. Common pathogens: Staph aureus, Pseudomonas, anaerobes (in wet gangrene). (6) Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) is a game-changer for complex wounds — if available. (7) When the wound is clean and granulating: consider split-thickness skin graft or biological dressings.
managementPearls.evidence: Armstrong DG, et al. Diabetic Foot Management. NEJM 2017; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: Exercise prescription — it's not just 'go walk'
managementPearls.pearl: The MIMIC and CLEVER trials proved that 'go home and walk more' is insufficient. A proper exercise prescription for claudication: (1) Walk on a flat surface (treadmill best) or a measured track. (2) Walk at a speed that brings on claudication pain in 3-5 minutes (3-4 km/h). (3) When pain reaches moderate (3-4/10 severity): STOP and rest until the pain resolves completely (usually 2-5 minutes). (4) Then walk again. Repeat: walk → rest → walk → rest for a total of 30-45 minutes per session. (5) Frequency: 3-5 sessions per week minimum. (6) Duration: at least 12 weeks — significant improvement is seen at 6-12 weeks. (7) Progression: as walking distance improves, increase speed or incline. (8) Mechanism: exercise improves collateral circulation (arteriogenesis), enhances endothelial function (eNOS upregulation), improves metabolic muscle efficiency. In Kenya, where few hospitals have supervised exercise programs, provide a simple written walking prescription and a pedometer (step counter) — the pedometer alone improves compliance and walking distance by 30%.
managementPearls.evidence: MIMIC Trial, Br J Surg 2008; CLEVER Trial, Circulation 2011; Gardner AW, et al. Exercise in PAD. Circulation 2011

managementPearls.context: Diagnosing PAD in the diabetic patient — the calcified vessel trap
managementPearls.pearl: Diabetic patients have medial calcinosis (Monckeberg's arteriosclerosis) — the tunica media calcifies, making arteries non-compressible. This causes a FALSELY ELEVATED ABI (>1.4). You think the circulation is normal, but the patient has critical ischemia. Solution: (1) Toe-brachial index (TBI) — digital arteries are spared from calcinosis. Normal TBI >0.7. CLTI <0.3. (2) Toe pressure (TP) — absolute TP <30 mmHg = CLTI. (3) TcPO2 <30 mmHg on the dorsum of the foot = CLTI. (4) If none of these are available (common in Kenya): clinical diagnosis — absent pedal pulses + dependent rubor + pallor on elevation + non-healing wound = CLTI regardless of ABI. Do not be falsely reassured by a normal ABI in a diabetic patient with foot ischemia. The mantra: 'ABI >1.4 in a diabetic = uninterpretable — measure toe pressure'.
managementPearls.evidence: Høyer C, et al. ABI vs TBI in diabetes. J Diabetes Complications 2016; ESVS Guidelines

operativeNuances.pitfall: Using a vein that is too small (<3 mm diameter), calcified, or sclerotic — leading to early graft failure
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always pre-operatively map the GSV with duplex ultrasound: measure diameter at the thigh and knee. Minimum acceptable diameter: 3 mm (distended). Use the REVERSED GSV for above-knee popliteal bypass — the larger end (at saphenofemoral junction) is anastomosed to the CFA. Use the IN SITU technique for below-knee popliteal or tibial bypass (better size matching, fewer size mismatches). If GSV is unavailable: arm vein (cephalic, basilic), SSV, or PTFE (preferably with heparin bonding and a distal vein cuff).

operativeNuances.procedure: Distal Tibial Bypass (to PT, AT, or Peroneal)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Poor distal target selection — bypassing to a calcified, diseased segment with poor runoff leads to graft thrombosis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Select the best tibial target based on CTA: ideally a segment with continuous runoff to the foot (PT > AT > Peroneal in order of preference). The target must be >2 mm in diameter and less than 50% calcified. Always dissect and palpate the target artery before committing — if it feels like a pipe (calcified), consider a different target or a peroneal bypass. For peroneal bypass: the peroneal artery does NOT directly supply the foot — you need a patent dorsalis pedis or PTA from the peroneal collaterals.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Angioplasty of long (>10 cm) tibial occlusions has poor patency (<40% at 1 year). Re-stenosis is common
operativeNuances.expertTip: For tibial angioplasty: use a low-profile (2.6F), steerable micro-catheter to cross the lesion. Predilate with a 2.0 mm balloon. Then use a DCB (drug-coated balloon with paclitaxel 3 µg/mm² — known to reduce restenosis by 50%). Inflate for 2 minutes (longer inflation reduces elastic recoil). Do NOT stent tibial arteries routinely (stent fractures, reocclusion). Bail-out stenting only for flow-limiting dissection (>50% stenosis) or residual stenosis >30% after DCB.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Performing a BKA when the patient has insufficient perfusion for BKA healing (30-50% BKA wounds fail to heal if ABI <0.3 or TP <30 mmHg)
operativeNuances.expertTip: Key decision: BKA vs AKA. BKA preserves the knee joint — much better for walking (uses 30% less energy than AKA). But BKA requires adequate blood supply for healing. Measure TP in the calf level skin: <30 mmHg — BKA will not heal. Alternatively, check tissue oximetry (StO2 <40% — poor healing). Clinical: bleeding skin edges — if no bleeding on incision, go to AKA. Do NOT close BKA primarily — leave the wound open with delayed primary closure at 5 days (reduces wound dehiscence). For AKA: myocutaneous flap (anterior = quadriceps, posterior = hamstrings) — preserve the longest possible lever arm.

operativeNuances.procedure: Common Femoral Artery Endarterectomy (CFA Patch Angioplasty)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Residual plaque at the distal endpoint (especially in the profunda femoris orifice) → early restenosis and recurrent symptoms within 3-6 months
operativeNuances.expertTip: CFA endarterectomy is done when the disease is confined to the CFA bifurcation (common + profunda + SFA origins). Technique: (1) Longitudinal arteriotomy from the distal external iliac artery (above the inguinal ligament) onto the CFA and extending 1-2 cm into the SFA and profunda. (2) Develop the endarterectomy plane in the media (circumferential). (3) Remove the entire plaque en bloc — it should come out as a 'Y-shaped' specimen. (4) The critical step: the distal endpoint in the profunda and SFA must be feathered — no intimal flap. If there is a flap, tack it down with 7-0 Prolene sutures. (5) Patch angioplasty: use Dacron, PTFE, bovine pericardium, or vein patch (GSV harvested from the ipsilateral thigh). For vein patch: harvest a 3 cm segment of GSV, open it longitudinally, and suture it to the arteriotomy with 5-0 or 6-0 Prolene. (6) Closure: the patch should create a wider-than-normal CFA bifurcation. Check flow with Doppler intra-operatively.

operativeNuances.procedure: Superficial Femoral Artery (SFA) Stenting — Avoiding Stent Fracture
operativeNuances.pitfall: The SFA is the most mobile artery in the body — it flexes, extends, and twists with every step and knee bend. Stent fracture rates of 20-50% have been reported with early-generation stents
operativeNuances.expertTip: To minimize stent fracture in the SFA: (1) Use self-expanding nitinol stents (NOT balloon-expandable — they crush). (2) Do NOT stent across the hip joint (proximal SFA) or the knee joint (distal SFA/popliteal) if possible. (3) For long segments (>15 cm), consider a 'spot stenting' strategy (stent only the most stenotic areas every 3-5 cm) rather than a 'full metal jacket' (one long continuous stent to cover the entire lesion). (4) Over-sizing: use a stent 1-2 mm larger than the reference vessel diameter. (5) Post-dilate consistently — underexpanded stents fracture more frequently. (6) Consider drug-coated balloons with bail-out stenting (stent only if flow-limiting dissection or residual stenosis >30%) — this is the current best practice. If a stent fracture occurs (symptomatic), re-stent with a more flexible, longer stent to bridge the fracture gap.

operativeNuances.procedure: Distal Vein Bypass — The Completion Angiogram
operativeNuances.pitfall: A technically perfect bypass can fail within hours because of a missed technical defect: valve cusp (in-situ bypass), intimal flap at anastomosis, or retained arteriovenous fistula
operativeNuances.expertTip: Completion angiography after distal bypass is MANDATORY — it detects technical defects in 15-20% of cases. Technique: (1) Access the proximal graft or native artery with a 21G butterfly needle. (2) Inject 10-20 mL of contrast and image the entire graft, both anastomoses, and runoff to the foot. (3) What to look for: smooth flow through the graft, no filling defects (intraluminal thrombus or valve cusp), patent distal anastomosis with good runoff (at least one named vessel to the foot, or a patent pedal arch). (4) If a defect is seen: if valve cusp — pass a valvulotome or 3F Fogarty; if intimal flap at anastomosis — re-open the anastomosis and place tacking sutures (7-0 Prolene); if AV fistula — ligate through a small stab incision. (5) A small defect that is 'not causing a significant stenosis' (<30% diameter loss) can be observed — but any defect >30% should be corrected before closure.

prognosis.mortality: PAD is a marker of severe systemic atherosclerosis. 5-year mortality: 30% for claudication, 50% for CLTI. 10-year mortality: 50% for claudication, 70% for CLTI. Causes: 65% CV (MI, stroke), 25% cancer, 10% other. CLTI patients have mortality similar to advanced cancer: 25% at 1 year. Amputation: 50% 2-year mortality after major amputation (the ultimate 'bad prognosis' marker). In Kenya, mortality may be higher due to limited CV risk management (statins, antiplatelets not widely available or affordable).
prognosis.morbidity: Amputation: CLTI has a 20-40% 1-year major amputation risk. After revascularization: amputation-free survival 60-75% at 1 year. Chronic pain: 50% have moderate-severe pain at rest or with walking. Wound complications: 30% of revascularized CLTI patients have non-healing wounds. Readmission: 30% within 30 days after CLTI revascularization (wound, reocclusion, amputation). Depression: 40% in CLTI patients.
prognosis.survival: Claudication: 5-year survival 70-80% (similar to moderate CAD). CLTI: 5-year survival 50-60% (worse than most cancers). After successful revascularization: 5-year survival 60-70%. After amputation: 5-year survival 30-40%. Causes of death: 50% CV (MI, CVA), 20% infection (wound, foot, sepsis), 10% VTE (PE), 20% other. Prevention of CV events with optimal medical therapy (statin, antiplatelet, smoking cessation) reduces mortality by 30-40%.
prognosis.recurrence: After revascularization: 1-year target vessel revascularization: 15-20% (endovascular) vs 5-10% (open vein bypass). 5-year patency: DCB/stent: 60-70%, vein bypass: 80%. Graft failure: 20-30% at 5 years for PTFE grafts. Contralateral PAD progression: 20% at 5 years require contralateral intervention. Amputation stump complications: 30% (wound dehiscence, infection, revision required). New CLTI development in contralateral limb: 15% at 5 years.
prognosis.reference: CAPRIE, Lancet 1996; COMPASS, NEJM 2017; VOYAGER-PAD, NEJM 2020; BASIL, Lancet 2005; BASIL-2, Lancet 2023; EUCLID, NEJM 2017
